text: The operation is performed through a left thoracotomy.
You can see initially the left superior intercostal vein is ligated and divided.
Once we've carried out this maneuver, we use diathermy in order to clear the mediastinal pleura from the thoracic aorta and its various vascular connections.
And you can see, as we do that, the branches of the aorta become clearer.
Once mobilized, the left subclavian artery is slung with silk.
The ductal remnant here, which wasn't patent, is coming into view, and at its base,
you can see the dilated diverticulum of comoral.
The ductal remnant is also slung with silk.

The duct is then transfixed with 6-o-pronein, preparing it for eventual ligation and division, and this is the first part of mobilizing the arch in order to prevent any further compression to the airways.
The aorta and its branches are mobilized further with sharp scissors dissection.
Once that's performed, it will also give a much clearer view of the posterior arch as it comes into continuity with the descending thoracic aorta.
And you can see that as the dissection is completed, the posterior arch is visible as it continues with the rest of the aorta.
A silk ligature is passed around the point of confluence of the left subclavian artery with the small left or anterior arch and the descending aorta.
The dilatation of the diverticulum of comoral is also found at this point.
The vascular clamp is then passed carefully around the base of the anterior arch.
This will have the effect of also excluding the diverticulum of comorel.
Once the clamp is applied, the diverticulum of comorel is closed with proline sutures at the point at which it communicates with the ductal remnant.
Once the vascular communications have been closed off with clamps or sutures, the anterior portion of the vascular ring is carefully divided with an 11 blade to free up the structures.
The vascular stumps on either side of the point of division are finally ligated, completing the hemostasis.
You can now see the completed operation with the vascular ring having been completely released.
Prior to chest closure, the mediastinal pleura over the aorta is closed.
